key_facts: Launched January 2015 by SPI Energy (NASDAQ: SPI) as China's first solar-focused online investment/leasing platform
Retail investors could buy solar modules that were then leased to PV projects, receiving yields of 6–8% annually
75% of project lessees were SPI-affiliated entities (related-party self-dealing)
Founder Peng Xiaofeng fled overseas under fraud investigation
Classic internet-finance (P2P) model applied to solar assets
Peaked during P2P internet finance boom (2015–2017)

failure_analysis.primary_cause: Related-party self-dealing: 75% of leased assets went to SPI's own affiliated entities, masking non-performing loans
failure_analysis.secondary_cause: Internet-finance regulatory crackdown (2018 P2P regulations eliminated the business model)
failure_analysis.tertiary_cause: Solar project cash-flows insufficient to service investor yields during subsidy delays
failure_analysis.governance_failure: Founder Peng Xiaofeng used retail investor capital to prop up SPI's own project pipeline; fled when enforcement came
failure_analysis.outcome: Platform shut down; investors suffered significant losses; SPI Energy stock collapsed; founder abroad as fugitive

business_model_notes: P2P solar finance platform: aggregate retail investor savings → deploy into SPI solar projects → return fixed yield + principal. Structurally identical to a Ponzi scheme when 75% of the 'independent' lessees were SPI affiliates. The model was commercially viable in theory (solar assets DO generate long-term cashflows) but governance failure destroyed it. Analogous to early LendingClub-style marketplace but without the credit separation.
historical_significance: First mover in China's solar-fintech space. Its collapse contributed to China's 2018 P2P regulatory crackdown that effectively wiped out online lending platforms. Documented warning case for: (1) related-party transactions in solar finance, (2) solar asset liquidity mismatch with short-term retail funding, (3) regulatory risk of P2P solar models.
research_notes: Highest confidence (★5). Fully documented in PRNewswire (2015 launch), 36Kr (platform collapse), People's Energy News (fraud investigation 2018-08-20). Wikipedia entry on SPI Energy documents corporate outcomes. Multiple independent sources with corroborating detail.
